texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The fascinating growth in clean and renewable energy to every corner of the world is essential for the global sustainability. The LIBs with their unique technical features (of: speci fi c energy density 100 -265 W h/kg, speci fi c power 250 -340 W/kg, life span cycles 400 -1200) have potentially revolutionized the market supply and demand of the renewable energy (Wagner, 2006; Choubey et al., 2016). Moreover, the development of parallel sectors such as, the direct plug-in hybrid vehicles, consumer electronics, and space, military and medical applications are creating new opportunities to cut down the cost of rechargeable LIBs and presenting them to be the suitable candidates in o ff -grid renewable energy for a common storage purpose (Diouf and Pode, 2015).

texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The uncertainty in production and utilization with the availability of lithium rich minerals and brine in a limited land area has grown the attention for the exploitation of those resources not coming under a speci fi c boundary. Approximately 230 billion tons of lithium in ubiquitous sea water and generation of a huge amount of used lithium ion batteries (spent LIBs) in di ff erent corner of the end-user countries can play a major role to change the scenario of securing lithium as a raw material for industry. Therefore, it needs to be exploited in such an e ff ective manner that it can assure the mitigation of projected supply risk of this energy-critical element. To explore the commercial viability for their sustainable exploitation, it is imperative to review the methodologies that have been investigated and have clear potential for upscaling.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This article deals with the processing of those resources that do not contributes yet to lithium supply in commodity market, and include the lithium recovery from sea water and spent LIBs. A purposive discussion on the electro-dialysis and reverse osmosis for the separation and recovery of lithium from sea water is of vital interest in future. A routinely disposed spent LIBs is a huge reservoir of lithium and need to be recovered as a main product along with the costlier cobalt, while recycling the spent LIBs. The various extraction and recovery processes of lithium are subdivided according to the adopted methodology, selection of lixiviant and obtaining the product in the desired form, whilst the technological and chemical perspective is also discussed. The deliberations on the edges and/or drawbacks, complications and prospects of the di ff erent processes are also included. It is believed that this state-of-the-art review can contribute in formulating the strategy for ful fi lling the global energy demand by a sustainable recovery of lithium from its non-conventional resources, and can provide future research directions.

texts.orig: The vital role of lithium in LIBs due to being a low density (0.534 g/ cm 3 ) metal with very high electrochemical potential ( -3.045 V) is therefore, accounted for their high economic and strategic importance while placing the lithium among ' energy-critical elements ' (Critical Materials Strategy, 2010; ECEs-Energy Critical Elements, 2011). As projected, lithium will witness a shortage in commodity market in near fi ve years and would be required 498 kilo tons of Li2CO3 in 2025 as compared to 265 kilo tons in 2015 (FOX-Davis Resources Specialist, 2013; Choubey et al., 2016). Over the last one year, the price of Li2CO3 per ton has already risen (in US dollar) from $4900 to $5900, and is predicted to rise further 20% by 2018 (Ker, 2015). The situation for the lithium in commodity market would be more critical with Tesala Giga factory planning to produce 5 Lacs electrical vehicles per year which alone requires the entire global production of LIBs at present

The huge gap between the market supplies against demand cannot be actualized without pushing the metallurgical recovery of lithium from all possible resources. Among the conventional resources, lithium production from minerals has been recorded to approximately 4420 tons in the year 2015 (USGS, 2015; Choubey et al., 2016) whereas, the contribution of brine remained nearly to 18,000 tons (USGS, 2014, 2015). Although the reserves are su ffi cient to ful fi l the market demands, commissioning of newer processing units and the enhanced product yield are urgently required (Choubey et al., 2016). The lithium production from brine is not easy due to already maximum production; whereas processing of minerals to produce lithium requires high cost investment. The non-availability of primary resources of lithium in every corner of the globe is also a major bottleneck.

texts.orig: The most advantageous aspect relating to such non-conventional resources of lithium is that they are not limited to a geographic boundary. Plenty of sea water is available across the country/region while the consumer country of LIBs is the owner of the spent LIBs as a potential resource of lithium, if recycled properly. However, the processing of sea water is very complicated largely due to low lithium contents, as low as 0.1 -0.2 ppm (Yoshizuka et al., 2006), whereas the problem remains associated with steps like physical bene fi ciation of spent LIBs has been identi fi ed to be critical. The aqueous solutions, either sea water or leach liquor in di ff erent media of the treated LIBs often containing high amount of impurities, are fraught with much di ffi culties during the downstream processing. Therefore, in order to explore new methodologies by seeking the improvement on separation and recovery of lithium values, on the both economic and technological front, it is vital to review the previous processing methods already in vogue or in the developmental stage.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Conventional techniques
texts.text: 2.1. Conventional techniques

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among the various techniques the solar evaporation, adsorption and co-precipitation of metal cations are the conventional routes of lithium enrichment. The solar evaporation technique is very simple with low cost operation; however, it requires prolonged time of more than six months to one year (Huang et al., 2010; Agha et al., 2002). The requirements of speci fi c weather conditions with high altitude are certainly the most critical parameters to be considered (Liu et al., 2014). The technique of the solar evaporation is almost the same as that of the brine described in part I of this review (Choubey et al., 2016), and hence excluded here. The co-precipitation is another technique which is often employed in the extraction of metals other than lithium, like, uranium, calcium etc. (Bardi, 2010; Chou and Mo ff att, 2000; Kelmers, 1980; Kim et al., 2003). Due to the lack of any direct exploitation of lithium, the co-precipitation methods are also excluded here, and only the adsorption by means of inorganic and organic adsorbents is considered for further discussion. A large number of inorganic adsorbents have been synthesized as presented in Fig. 4 with works on the surface modi fi cation to achieve higher selectivity among the metal cations, whereas only a few reports exist on the use of organic ion-exchangers/ resins; details on both types of resins are discussed below.

texts.orig: 2.1.1. Inorganic adsorbents
texts.text: 2.1.1. Inorganic adsorbents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.1.1.1. Adsorption with manganese oxide . The spinel-type manganese oxide has been preferentially used in lithium adsorption from sea water. The formation of a three dimensional structure with lithium as LiMn2O4 compound favors the sorption mechanism instead of a two dimensional layered crystal structure of LiMnO2. The smaller size of Li-ions compared to any other alkali and alkaline metals supports to form a stable structure of LiMn2O4, whereas in LiMnO2 lithium occupied an interlayer octahedral site (Ooi et al., 1991; Nazri and Pistoia, 2003). However, ionic radius of magnesium is almost similar to lithium, but the degree of hydration for Mg > Li increases the overall size of hydrated magnesium, thereby hindering the sorption of Mg in alkaline pH (> 8.0). The adsorbed lithium can be eluted with dil. HCl to obtain the pure LiCl solution (Ooi et al., 1991) to yield ∼ 33% recovery of lithium by crystallization (Yoshizuka et al., 2006). The formation of a three dimensional structure with lithium as LiMn2O4 compound favors the sorption mechanism instead of a two dimensional layered crystal structure of LiMnO2. The smaller size of Li-ions compared to any other alkali and alkaline metals supports to form a stable structure of LiMn2O4, whereas in LiMnO2 lithium occupied an interlayer octahedral site (Ooi et al., 1991; Nazri and Pistoia, 2003). texts.orig: Kitajou et al. (2003) made a novel λ -type spinel manganese dioxide by treating the Li1.5Mn2O4 with dil. HCl and used to enrich the lithium content from10 ppm (of sea water) to 400 times higher concentration at an equilibrium pH 8.1. A membrane-type spinel manganese oxide was prepared using polyvinyl chloride (PVC), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and Li1.33Mn1.67O4 as a precursor powder. Prepared spinel-type lithium manganese oxide exhibited the selectivity due to narrow sites of adsorbent that allowed to pass only the Li-ions (Umeno et al., 2002). The large ionic size of Na + , K + and Ca 2+ than Li + su ff ers a large steric hindrance to enter into the sorption sites (Wajima et al., 2012). The selectivity between Mg 2+ and Li + of similar ionic radius can be corroborated with a four folds higher free energy of hydration for Mg 2+ than that of the Li + . Therefore, it requires a high energy for Mgions dehydration to support their entry via the sorption sites.

texts.orig: A number of adsorbents have been used for lithium recovery as summarized in Table 2. The manganese oxide (H1.6Mn1.6O4) prepared from precursor of Li1.6Mn1.6O4 by two di ff erent methods, the hydrothermal and re fl ux, showed a lithium uptake capacity of 40 mg/g and 34.1 mg/g, respectively. Adsorbent prepared by the re fl ux methods had lower sorption capacity than that prepared by the hydrothermal method which is attributed to the less crystalline structure of the adsorbent (Chitrakar et al., 2001). A synthesized polymeric reservoir system containing Li1.33Mn1.67O4 as sorbent having the properties of similar pore size with the ionic size of lithium, su ffi cient mechanical strength, and chemically resistant to HCl solution, the hydrophilic characteristics of pores could adsorb > 90% Li in 24 h followed by an easy desorption in a 0.5 mol/L HCl solution (Chung et al., 2008). Hong et al. (2015) used the hydrogen manganese oxide (HMO) powder and immobilized HMO on alpha-alumina bead (AAB) for the recovery of lithium. Immobilized HMO exerts higher lithium adsorption capacity than the HMO powder, because immobilized HMO on AAB forms thin layer and widely dispersed on the AAB surface, that increase the contact of solution thoroughly. This results in the enhanced lithium adsorption. HMO/AAB adsorbs 21.7 mg/g from seawater in the pH range 7 -8, whereas, HMO powder shows less adsorption capacity of 13.4 mg/g. Ryu et al. (2015b) examined the e ff ect of shapes using cylindrical and powdered lithium manganese oxide (LMO). The structural block in cylinder-type LMO showed a lower adsorption of lithium (15.06 mg/g) than the powder-LMO (27.62 mg/g). However, the sorption capacity of cylindrical-LMO increased up to 19 mg/g when 7 wt.% of Na2CO3 was introduced to increase the internal channel and pore volume in the LMO. Furthermore, the pressure e ff ect on lithium adsorption was examined in a multi-stage column (MSC). Pressure accelerated the rate of adsorption due to an increase in desorbate-sorbent contact to achieve the sorption capacity of ∼ 31 mg/g while applying a 6 kg·f·cm -2 pressure to the system.

texts.orig: 2.1.1.2. Adsorption with titanium-based sorbents . Abe and Chitrakar (1987) used titanium(IV) antimonite cation exchanger (TiSbA) to adsorb the lithium from sea water containing 0.16 ppm Li and hydrothermal water with 12.2 ppm Li. For the hydrothermal water, the equilibrium was attained in 10 h with a sorption capacity of 0.086 meq/g at 30 °C and 0.09 meq/g at 90 °C. It however, required a prolonged period of 168 h to attain the equilibrium for sea water at 30 °C with a sorption capacity of 0.076 meq/g. More than 90% lithium was recovered by desorption with 0.1 M HNO3. Higher sorption in alkaline medium as compared to that of the acidic/neutral solution was however, achieved by Shi et al. (2013) with a sorption capacity of 39.8 mg/g and 30.9 mg/g from LiOH and LiCl solutions, respectively after 192 h. Such a phenomenon was also supported by using the H2TiO3 {synthesized by CH3COOLi and Ti(OC4H9)4 using sol gel process followed by HCl treatment} and TiO2 nanotube of diameter 50 -70 nm and 1 -2 µm length synthesized by a soft hydrothermal method at 150 °C for 48 h, where 31.2 mg/g and 39.4 mg/g Li could be adsorbed, respectively at alkaline pH (Moazeni et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2016). texts.orig: 2.1.1.3. Other adsorbents . Kaneko and Takahashi (1990) used a binary alumina-magnesia gel for selective adsorption of lithium based on the cation-exchange reaction between Li + of sea water and the monovalent cations located onto the gel surfaces with a maximum loading capacity of 100 µg/g in between pH range 7 -8 could enrich the lithium concentration to seven folds. Spinel Li-Sb-Mn composite oxide having di ff erent molar ratios of Sb/Mn: 0.05, 0.33 and 1.0 was also used for lithium sorption (Wen et al., 2011). A molar ratio of Sb/Mn: 0.05 exhibited higher lithium adsorption at an equilibrium pH ∼ 12 with the adsorption capacity of 33.23 mg/g. An increase in Sb/Mn molar ratio causes the cell expansion leading to decreased lithium adsorption. Recently, Ryu et al. (2015a) proposed the electrostatic fi eld assistance (EFA) adsorption using lithium selective electrode to reduce the time required for upgrading the lithium concentration over the conventional adsorption method. Adsorption performance of the lithium selective electrode increased by 3 times when EFA was applied (1V) which accelerated the rate of reaction while achieving the equilibrium in 1000 min at pH 8.6 as compared to 3000 min required to achieve the equilibrium for physical adsorption at pH 8.9. In addition electrostatic fi eld assistance (EFA) adsorption also showed a good reproducibility and durability for the repeated adsorption/desorption cycle without needing the acidic solutions for desorption of lithium. Jeong et al. (2015) investigated the lithium adsorption behavior onto activated carbon (AC) and compared with a surface modi fi ed carbon (SMAC). AC showed a low sorption capacity for lithium (only 0.28 wt.%), which could be enhanced to 0.78 wt.% with the SMAC (modi fi ed by KOH treatment of AC). The negative charge of O-atoms generated onto AC surface in fl uenced the Li + sorption by the electrostatic interaction forces between them. Therefore, the adsorption capacity increased with respect to the increasing concentration of KOH used for AC surface modi fi cation. In contrast, the AC modi fi ed by phosphoric acid did not show any in fl uence on the sorption capacity (Jeong et al., 2015).

texts.text: 2.1.2. Organic adsorbents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The organic adsorbents comprising of ion exchange resins, have been employed in some studies for lithium separation and enrichment from sea water (Suzuki et al., 2015). Guo et al. (2013) used the resins of nomenclature LSC-100, LSC-500 and D001 which correspond to their functional groups of -N(CH2COO -) 2, -NHCH2PO 2 -3, and SO3 -, respectively. The study shows that the resins LSC-100 and LSC-500 were highly selective for lithium extraction with the adsorption capacity ≥ 5.5 mmol/ g over the other alkali metal ions by following the extraction order as: Li + ≫ K + ∼ Rb + ∼ Cs + , and Li + ≫ K + > Rb + ∼ Cs + , respectively. The authors described that the strong ligands of LSC-100 and LSC-500 preferred the strong complexation with lithium than other alkali metals. In contrast, the resin D001 followed a reverse trend of extraction in which Cs + was adsorbed fi rst and lithium in the last with an adsorption capacity ≥ 4.3 mmol/g. The separation factor for D001 followed the order: β Cs/Li > β Rb/Li > β K/Li. Such a separation phenomenon of D001 was corroborated with the hard and soft acid base (HSAB) principle and the electrostatic attractions between the -SO3 functional groups and metal ions (Sakuma and Kawamura, 2011; Tansel et al., 2006; Tansel, 2012). As the ionic radius in hydrated form of metals increases from Cs + to Li + , the Cs + ions can reach closely to the negatively charged surface of the resin D001 resulting in the strong electrostatic attraction between them.

texts.orig: Some researchers have employed the solvent extraction techniques in lithium separation from sea water using di ff erent organic extractants as briefed in Table 3. Nevertheless the extraction faces di ffi culties due to the tendency to form a stable hydrated species of lithium with water molecules. Hence, the solvent extraction of lithium is usually being done at the equilibrium pH>7. Kinugusa et al. (1994), Ma et al. (2000) used the synergistic extractant comprising of LIX 54 abbreviated as HR and TOPO (as S) in a di ff erent proportions diluted in kerosene (0.015 mol/L LIX54 and 0.02 mol/L TOPO; 0.146 mol/L LIX 54 with 0.05 mol/L TOPO) to explain the lithium extraction mechanism within the equilibrium range pH 11 -13, as given in Eqs. (7) -(10).

texts.orig: C11ph β diketone and 0.1 mol/L T2EPH and 0.1 M C11ph β diketone and 0.1 mol/ L TBP. Among the four synergistic extractants examined only two mixtures (0.1 mol/L C11ph β diketone and 0.1 mol/L TOPO; 0.1 mol/L C11ph β diketone and 0.1 mol/L TBPO) showed the extractability of above 99% Li at a pH near 12. The other two organic mixtures yielded a lower e ffi ciency (< 50%) at the same extraction pH ( ∼ 12). As the equilibrium pH increased to 13, the extraction increased up to 90%, indicating the hydrophilic nature of the lithium species with water molecule. However, to maintain the higher pH addition of ammonia solution instead of NaOH was suggested to avoid any competition between the Na + and Li + ions during extraction. A synergistic mixture of thenoyl tri fl uoroacetone (H-TTA) and 1, 10phenanthroline (Phen) system yielded the highest lithium extraction of > 74% (Ishimori et al., 2002). Mixing TOPO with H-TTA however, showed very poor extraction of Li (4.3%) at the same equilibrium pH (11.6). Using the di ff erent diluents, chloroform, methyl-isobutyl ketone (MIBK), n -hexane, m -xylene, and benzene in TTA and TOPO mixture, Kim et al. (2003) found that lithium formed the stable complex at pH ∼ 9 while using the non-polar benzene as the diluent. Recently Pranolo et al. (2015) investigated the applicability for synergistic extraction of lithium from the alkaline solutions. Approximately 97% lithium with a little sodium was extracted at pH 11 while using the extractant mixture 0.4 mol/L LIX 54 + 0.2 mol/L Cyanex 923 diluted in ShellSol. The co-extracted sodium was scrubbed with de-ionized water and the lithium was subsequently stripped with 0.17 mol/L HCl yielding lithium chloride solution. Harvianto et al. (2015) used the synergistic mixture of the noyltri fl uoroacetone and trioctylphosphine oxide (0.02 M TTA + 0.04 M TOPO) in a pH range of 8 -12 for the maximum 90% extraction of lithium in 80 min equilibration time. The back-extraction of lithium was performed with HCl at pH 2 -4 to get pure solutions of LiCl.

Among the four synergistic extractants examined only two mixtures (0.1 mol/L C11ph β diketone and 0.1 mol/L TOPO; 0.1 mol/L C11ph β diketone and 0.1 mol/L TBPO) showed the extractability of above 99% Li at a pH near 12. The other two organic mixtures yielded a lower e ffi ciency (< 50%) at the same extraction pH ( ∼ 12). As the equilibrium pH increased to 13, the extraction increased up to 90%, indicating the hydrophilic nature of the lithium species with water molecule. However, to maintain the higher pH addition of ammonia solution instead of NaOH was suggested to avoid any competition between the Na + and Li + ions during extraction. A synergistic mixture of thenoyl tri fl uoroacetone (H-TTA) and 1, 10phenanthroline (Phen) system yielded the highest lithium extraction of > 74% (Ishimori et al., 2002). Mixing TOPO with H-TTA however, showed very poor extraction of Li (4.3%) at the same equilibrium pH (11.6). texts.orig: Due to the limited sorption capacity after a prolonged operation, non-e ffi cient regeneration of adsorbents and handling of a large volume with plenty of impurities and minute lithium contents, electro-dialysis is emerging as a prominent technique for lithium exploitation from sea water. It is an electrochemical separation process in which ions are transferred through semi-permeable membrane under the in fl uence of electric potential. It consists of one feed compartment and another concentrate compartment separated, by an anion and a cation exchange membrane placed between the two electrodes. Generally, multiple electro-dialysis cells are arranged into a con fi guration called an electro-dialysis stack with two types of ion exchange membrane i.e. cation exchange membrane which allows only positively charged ions to pass through the membrane and anion exchange membrane which allows only the negatively charged ions to pass through membrane. Hoshino and Terai (2011a,b) used the lithium permeable ionic liquid impregnated membrane (ILIM) named PP13- TFSI-n-methyl-n-propylpiperidiumbis (tri fl orometanesulfonyl) imide) organic membrane LI-iOM and a high durability LI-i-OM. Both sides of the LI-i-OM were covered with a Na fi on 324 overcoat to prevent the loss of impregnated ionic liquid to improve the durability of LI-i-OM on prolong electrodialysis. Ionic liquid permeates only Li + due to the presence of TFSI functional group which exhibits high conductivity toward Li + as shown in Fig. 6a. 5.94% and 22.2% lithium was concentrated as LiCl in cathode side using the IL-i-OM and IL-i-OM respectively, then Li + and Cl -were separated using cation and anion exchange membranes at the applied voltage of 18V in 1 h (Hoshino, 2013a). Further Hoshino (2013b) used the ion selective SELEMION TM CSO membrane to separate the mono and divalent ions (Fig. 6b). This membrane permeates only monovalent ions to pass from one side (anode) to another side (cathode) while divalent ions remain on the anodic side under the applied potential of 3V. texts.orig: TMPA-TFSI ionic liquid, because it exhibited lower conductivity for Li + than the others such as Na + and K + . Thus lithium gets concentrated on anodic compartment with ∼ 63% survival ratio after 11 h (scheme shown in Fig. 6c). Another ILIM impregnated with IL PP13-TFSI allows transport of Li + from anode to cathode with > 22% recovery of lithium (Hoshino, 2013a). Lithium was separated using cation exchange membrane that permits the lithium ions to permeate while permitting the permeation of Cl -ions through an anion exchange membrane. Approximately, 95% lithium separation was achieved with 0.1 M HCl at the applied voltage 18 V in 1. Further studies were made to replace the ILIM by the lithium ionic superconductor (LISM) using the mobility of Li + (Hoshino, 2015), which exhibited a higher durability. The LISM allowed only the Li + ions to pass leaving other cations (Na + , Mg 2+ , Ca 2+ and K + ) in one side. The lithium recovery ratio increased with time, where the requirement of power supply was ful fi lled by the electricity generated during the process itself. The principle of Li permeation by LISM is likely to be similar to that of a concentration cell in which the driving force of lithium permeation being the di ff erential Li-ion concentrations between the negative and positive electrode sides. The process shows fairly good energy e ffi ciency, easy to scale up, and seems feasible to commercialize in the near future.

texts.orig: A selective enrichment of lithium over other alkali metals by transport phenomena using Crown nitrophenol is considered to transfer Li + selectively from an aqueous source phase (basic) to an aqueous receiving phase (acidic) with the counter transport of proton (Sakamoto et al., 1987). At the basic interface, the nitrophenol group loses a proton and concurrently the adjacent crown ether binds a Li + . The crown-Li + complex those are electrically neutralized intra molecularly get di ff used to the acidic interface through the membrane. In contrast at the acidic interface, the crown complex is forced to release the Li + by the protonation of the anionic site, and therefore loses the negative charge. During this process pH of the source solution and receiving phase can play an important role for transport of the Li ions. Li + transport from source phase to receiving phase was very di ffi cult up to pH 11. But as the pH of source phase increased to > 11, it enhanced the Li + transport by the promotion of Li + complex formation at the interface for the source phase. On other hand, increasing the pH of the receiving phase above pH 6 lowers the Li + transportability by depressing the cation release of the complex at the interface for the receiving phase.

texts.orig: Recently, Hwang et al. (2016) compared the chemical desorption of lithium with electro-dialysis system. It was found that 85% Li was desorbed under the following conditions in electro-dialysis system: pH less than 4; the number of sheets of bipolar membranes: 4; voltage per sheet: 6.5 V, and fl ow rate: 0.44 mL/cm 2 /min, time: 180 min; whereas the same amount of lithium was desorbed in 360 min by the chemical desorption process.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Processes for lithium recovery from the spent LIBs
texts.text: 3. Processes for lithium recovery from the spent LIBs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Spent LIBs are a valuable source of critical metals like cobalt (5 -33%), lithium (5 -7%), manganese (15 -20%) and nickel (0.02 -0.3%), and therefore, they have been identi fi ed as one of the potential resources for lithium production in near future. Numerous works have been carried out in this area to recover the contained metals and recycle the spent LIBs. It may be noted that some of the developed processes are commercialized, but few of them recover lithium (Shin et al., 2005; Xu et al., 2008). Mostly the cobalt is being recovered which is rarer than the lithium abundance. The signi fi cant amount of lithium (5 -7 wt.%) present in the spent LIBs makes it very attractive as compared to the lithium concentration in sea water (0.1 -0.2 ppm) (Kesler et al., 2012; Brown, 2010; Yoshizuka et al., 2006). At the same time, handling the limited impurity pro fi le of spent LIBs is much easier than its minerals (which require high calori fi c pre-treatment), and brines and sea water which are time consuming. Hence in this review, spent LIBs have been considered as the major reservoir of lithium and the process applicable for lithium recovery are being discussed below.

texts.orig: consists of oxide of Li and Co (LiCoO2) coated on the aluminum foil. Polyvinylidene fl uoride (PVDF), butadiene-styrene copolymer or modi fi ed cellulose are used as the supporting substance to bind graphite and mixed oxide powder. The liquid electrolytes which consist of lithium salts, such as, LiPF6/LiBF4/LiClO4 in an organic solvent, such as ethylene carbonate, dimethyl carbonate and diethyl carbonate etc., also acts as a carrier between the positive and negative electrodes when current fl ows through an external circuit. Therefore, physical treatment is necessary to separate all components of LIBs and enrichment of black mass (which contains metallic values of concern) prior to subsequent hydrometallurgical treatment. Gratz et al. (2014) have reported that the spent LIBs must be discharged prior to pre-treatment to avoid any short circuit due to the presence of fl ammable electrolytes therein. Immersion in salt solution/ distilled water (might be in presence of Fe powder)/ liquid nitrogen is commonly used to deactivate the LIBs (Sonoc et al., 2015; Dorella and Mansur, 2007). Sometimes, internal short-circuit can be invoked by pressing the LIBs under the closed condition.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2.1. Mechanical and mechanochemical treatment
texts.text: 3.2.1. Mechanical and mechanochemical treatment

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mechanical treatment of spent LIBs improves the metallic concentration which consequently reduces the need for leachate puri fi cation in the hydrometallurgical processes. For this, multistage crushing and sieving have been practiced to enrich the metal values as shown in Fig. 7 (pre-treatment-I). During the mechanical treatment, the strength and tenacity of the cathode materials cause the release of harmful gases (Shin et al., 2005). Employing a mechanical separation, the mixed composition of metals, organic and inorganic matters, and their complex nature can present di ffi culties in the separation of components accurately from each other. Therefore, the mechanical treatment is not found to be an eco-friendly process in the recycling of spent LIBs (Shin et al., 2005). A combination of mechanical crushing and grinding process with the dissolution of binder and supporting substrates in hot N-methyl pyrolidene (NMP) showed an enhanced liberation of electrode materials (Contestabile et al., 2001). Further, a few studies have also been made on the mechanochemical treatment for lithium recovery with two types of application in, (i) the milling followed by a separate leaching process (dry milling, DM), and (ii) simultaneous milling and leaching process (wet milling, WM) (Ou et al., 2015; Saeki et al., 2001). In the presence of air, LiCoO2 was ground in a planetary ball mill with the polyvinyl chloride (as a source of chloride) to form the chlorides of lithium and cobalt products, which can easily be extracted during the water leaching. Alternatively, the change in crystalline structure of the LiCo0.2Ni0.8O2 has been carried out by the mechanochemical treatment with and without alumina (Zhang et al., 2000). Mixing of alumina changed the crystalline structure to amorphous, thus enhancing the extraction e ffi ciency of metals to above 90% (Xu et al., 2008). The employed dry milling of LIBs was found to be advantageous in separation of the magnetic materials and steel case. This helped in concentrating the target metals in the black mass with an increased extraction e ffi cacy in the leaching stage.

Later on the slurry made with nitric acid for the leaching of LiCoO2 was placed in a stainless-steel crucible and calcined in presence of air at 500 -1000 °C as shown in Fig. 7 (pre-treatment-II). In another process, one part of the active mass (cathode + anode + electrolyte) of LIBs was mixed with eight fold of the KHSO4 in a mu ffl e furnace at 500 °C for 300 min (Paulino et al., 2008). The heat treatment was done under air fl ow to avoid the reduction of sulfate into SO2 and sul fi de species. As compared to this, calcination of active mass at 500 °C for 300 min has found to enhance lithium extraction in water leaching. This was mainly because of the fact that the carbon acts as a sorbent which was entirely removed by calcination. Incineration of active mass of the spent LIBs at higher temperature of 900 °C however, has showed the low recovery of cobalt in leaching without a ff ecting the dissolution e ffi ciency of lithium in sulfuric acid solution (Shin et al., 2005). Lowering down the incineration temperature to < 700 °C has successfully enhanced the cobalt leaching along with the entire lithium extraction into the sulfuric acid (Petranikova et al., 2011). Recently Hanisch et al. (2015) performed the thermal decomposition of the polyvinylidene fl uoride (PVDF) binder material using the combined techniques of thermal treatment and mechanical separation, called as adhesion neutralization via incineration and impact liberation (ANVIIL). To determine the suitable temperature for decomposition of PVDF material, thermogravimetric analyses were carried out in a range of temperature from 350 to 650 °C. The decomposition of polymeric PVDF binder started at 350 °C under the oxygen atmosphere. When the temperature was increased to 550 °C, almost 98.85 wt.% of the binder mass was volatized in 20 min. Further, increase in temperature resulted in beginning of loss of carbon black followed by the loss of graphite at 650 °C. Therefore, a temperature range of 500 -580 °C can be considered ideal for the PVDF decomposition while avoiding degradation of other materials. The main drawback of the calcination process for current battery binders is the expulsion of potentially unhealthy incineration gases. Chen et al. (2015a) reported the thermal treatment process to decompose PVDF completely at 550 °C but below the melting point of Al foils (650 °C). During the heat-treatment the redox reactions also occur between the cathode conductor acetylene black and the active cathode materials, LiNi1/3Co1/3Mn1/3O2, resulting in partial reduction of the active cathode materials from a high charge to a low charge state, facilitating the high recovery of metals during leaching.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The thermal treatment of spent LIBs has been found to be a straightforward and convenient operation. At the same time, the need of equipments for cleaning the o ff -gases and smokes resulting from the combustion of carbon and organic compounds are disadvantageous to increase the processing cost. Looking at such disadvantages, some researchers have recently emphasized on pyrolysis instead of mechanical and thermal treatment of spent LIBs. Sun and Qiu (2011) used pyrolysis techniques for the separation of cathode active material from aluminum foils at 600 °C because most of the binders could be evaporated or decomposed completely at this temperature. Above 600 °C, the aluminum foils become fragile and separation of cathode active material becomes very di ffi cult. This phenomenon becomes still more serious when the temperature reaches 700 °C. Furthermore, the aluminum foils may remain intact with cathode active material due to di ff erent adhesive agents used and methods of making cathodes, but the phenomenon of peeling is an inevitable outcome. Vacuum pyrolysis has noticeable advantages over mechanical treatment (Benallal et al., 1995). Besides preventing the evolution of toxic gases and lowering the decomposition temperature of organic compounds, the oxidation of metals can be restricted. Consequently, it is not only the cobalt and lithium that can be e ffi ciently recycled, but the pyrolysis products or volatiles from electrolyte (e.g., LiPF6) and binder (e.g., PVDF) could also be collected and reused. Pyrolyzed products were also characterized of by FT-IR which indicates that almost all the PVDF materials are collected from the condenser and can be recycled (Sun and Qiu, 2012). After the pre-treatment, the hydrometallugical process consisting of leaching, solvent extraction, precipitation, etc. can be employed to recover the lithium values along with costlier cobalt.

texts.orig: 3.2.3. Ultrasonic cleaning
texts.text: 3.2.3. Ultrasonic cleaning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Instead of physical and mechanical pre-treatment, a few studies have been reported to separate the cathode material using ultrasonic treatment in presence of solvents such as N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), N-N dimethylformamide (DMF), N-N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC), N-N-dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and ethanol. By this, the binder materials (PVDF) get dissolved under the conditions: temperature 60 °C, ultrasonic power 240 W, time 30 min, and S/L:1/10. By following the theory of '' like dissolves like ' , the polar compound (PVDF) gets easily dissolved in the polar solvent. The NMP, a strong polar solvent has shown the largest solubility resulting in achieving the highest peel-o ff e ffi ciency of the cathode materials, whereas ethanol (a weak polar solvent) hardly dissolves the PVDF with the lowest peel-o ff e ffi ciency as shown in Fig. 8 (He et al., 2015).
texts.text: Instead of physical and mechanical pre-treatment, a few studies have been reported to separate the cathode material using ultrasonic treatment in presence of solvents such as N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), N-N dimethylformamide (DMF), N-N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC), N-N-dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and ethanol. By this, the binder materials (PVDF) get dissolved under the conditions: temperature 60 °C, ultrasonic power 240 W, time 30 min, and S/L:1/10. By following the theory of '' like dissolves like ' , the polar compound (PVDF) gets easily dissolved in the polar solvent. The NMP, a strong polar solvent has shown the largest solubility resulting in achieving the highest peel-o ff e ffi ciency of the cathode materials, whereas ethanol (a weak polar solvent) hardly dissolves the PVDF with the lowest peel-o ff e ffi ciency as shown in Fig. 8 (He et al., 2015).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Peeling of cathode materials from aluminum can be explained by two di ff erent mechanisms. In the fi rst, ultrasound promotes the convective motion of solvent, thus enhancing the dissolution rate of PVDF and increasing the peel-o ff e ffi ciency of the cathode materials (He et al., 2015). The second can be described as the ultrasonic cavitation (Azar, 2009; Li et al., 2014; Oz and Yarimtepe, 2014). When the ultrasonic starts to work, the acoustic pressure undergoes a compression and rarefaction cycle. During the rarefaction stage, the pressure in the solvent molecule becomes negative, and the ultrasound may cause the formation of vacuum holes in the solvent. The gases dissolved in the solvent then enter into these holes, resulting in the formation of millions of cavitation bubbles, and the cavitation bubbles further expand as more gases enter into the vacuum holes. During the compression stage, the cavitation bubbles burst and cause a high amount of impact force on the interface of cathode and solvent around the bubbles, causing the cathode materials to peel o ff from the Al foils, as illustrated in Fig. 9. Therefore, the e ffi cient peeling of the cathode materials from Al foils was a result of the dissolution of PVDF combined with the cavitation caused by the ultrasound.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Extraction of metal values from the spent LIBs into aqueous solutions
texts.text: 3.3. Extraction of metal values from the spent LIBs into aqueous solutions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After enrichment of metallic values from spent LIBs using the above mentioned techniques, various methods have been explored to extract the metal values in aqueous solutions of divergent media. Conventionally, cobalt remained as the focused metal to extract which has been reviewed earlier (Meshram et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2008). As this review deals with the lithium recovery, therefore, the studies related to lithium recovery (along with cobalt) are being considered within the scope of this review paper. To recover the concerned metal/s via hydrometallurgical route, extracting them into the aqueous solutions using a suitable lixiviant viz., the leaching is the major step after the pre-treatment of spent LIBs. There are di ff erent types of lixiviants, such as, mineral acids/ organic acids/ alkali solutions which have been used for the leaching studies.

texts.orig: The leaching e ffi cacy of lithium decreased to ∼ 80% with 2 mol/L HCl in 3 h with the addition of H2O2 as a reductant. To enhance the leaching e ffi ciency (>80% Li) with the same amount of reagent consumption, it took 18 h duration at a higher temperature. Alternatively, second stage leaching of unleached residue could also increase the overall extraction e ffi ciency. The obtained leach liquor contains metals like, Co, Ni and Li which undergoes to several puri fi cation steps to yield the pure value-added product of lithium as the carbonate salt. In a similar manner Wang et al. (2009, 2012) have used 4 mol/L HCl to leach > 99% Co, Mn, Li and Ni at 80 °C in 60 min keeping the pulp density of 20 g/L. But to obtain the same leaching e ffi ciency in the sulfuric acid medium, a prolonged leaching of 180 min was required along with the conditions such as acid concentration: 3 mol/L H2SO4, temperature: 90 °C, pulp density: 66.6 g/L and Na2S2O3 (reductant): 0.25 mol/L. The comparative fast kinetics in HCl leaching may probably be attributed to the ability of chloride ions to destabilize the surface layer. The authors have however, shown the similar phenomenon occurring during the leaching of various metals as observed with H2O2 as the reductant. Interestingly the lithium extraction remained more or less the same in the absence or presence of the reductant. By maintaining the other parameters as: 1 mol/L H2SO4, 95 °C temperature and 240 min duration, the leaching e ffi ciency was 93.4% Li, 66.2% Co, 96.3% Ni and 50.2% Mn at a pulp density of 50 g/L in the absence of sodium bisul fi te. When 0.075 mol/ L sodium bisul fi te was used as a reductant leaching e ffi ciency reached to 96.7% Li, 91.6% Co, 96.4% Ni and 87.9% Mn at a pulp density of 20 g/ L.

texts.text: 3.3.2. Leaching with organic acids

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The recovery routes for the metals of interest from spent LIBs by leaching with mineral acids is well-established, however releasing of the toxic and harmful gases like, Cl2, SO3 and NOx are of serious concern from the environmental point of view. Hence, exploring the metal recovery by leaching with organic acids seems attractive which of late is being extensively studied by various researchers (Table 4). For this mainly the malic acid (C4H6O5), oxalic acid (H ₂ C ₂ O ₄ ), succinic acid (C4H6O4), aspartic acid (C4H7NO4), and citric acid (C6H8O7) have been employed (Li et al., 2010a, 2010b, 2012, 2013). All the lithium along with 90% cobalt could be leached out with either 1.25 mol/L citric acid +1vol.% H2O2 or, 1.5 mol/L malic acid + 2 vol.% H2O2 at a pulp density of 20 g/L and 90 °C. It was also observed that going above 2 mol/L concentration of the malic acid has no further in fl uence on the leaching e ffi ciency, because the conductivity of 2 mol/L malic acid is highest at a temperature ∼ 100 °C with the higher rate for ion-transfer. All the lithium along with 90% cobalt could be leached out with either 1.25 mol/L citric acid +1vol.% H2O2 or, 1.5 mol/L malic acid + 2 vol.% H2O2 at a pulp density of 20 g/L and 90 °C. It was also observed that going above 2 mol/L concentration of the malic acid has no further in fl uence on the leaching e ffi ciency, because the conductivity of 2 mol/L malic acid is highest at a temperature ∼ 100 °C with the higher rate for ion-transfer. The reaction involved during the malic acid leaching is as follows

(2015) have used strong organic acid such as oxalic acid to attack on the strong chemical bond between the lithium and cobalt of LiCoO2 from the spent LIBs. By the addition of H2O2, the rate of leaching could be enhanced by the higher replacement rate of Li + of LiCoO2 with H + ions of oxalic acid and at the same time Co 3+ is reduced to Co 2+ . texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4. Separation and purification of leach liquors
texts.text: 3.4. Separation and purification of leach liquors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The obtained leach liquors of LIBs undergo separation and puri fi cation steps for the removal of metal ion impurities therein. The impurity removal steps can be divided into two parts from the operational viewpoint. The impurities like, Cu, Mn, Fe and Al are usually removed prior to separate the Co and Ni from the Li-bearing solutions. Such a division is often based on the characteristics of the particular metal ion impurities like, the order of stability, their hydrolysis constant, coordination chemistry, the role of d-orbital in the metal complexation, reaction thermodynamics etc. (Habashi, 1969; Jun et al., 2016). Due to these characteristics, the solvent extraction of lithium from the leach solution of LIBs is not much favored. The hydrated species of lithium ions have a more stable structure than water itself that preferentially keeps lithium into the aqueous solutions. Hence, lithium extraction from aqueous solutions requires the kind of extractants which is more basic than water (Lee, 1961). This can be achieved by getting the synergistic e ff ect of solvents usually at higher equilibrium pH (> 6) as discussed in Section 2.3. The extraction of metals like Cu, Al, Co, Ni, Fe, and Mn from the leach solutions of LIBs does not face such kind of di ffi culties and can be easily extracted at pH ≤ 6.5 while leaving lithium in the ra ffi nate.

texts.orig: 3.4.1. Separation of Cu, Fe, Al and Mn
texts.text: 3.4.1. Separation of Cu, Fe, Al and Mn

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 3.4.1.1. Using solvent extraction . A leach liquor (16.9 g/L Co, 3.8 g/L Li, 0.6 g/L Fe, 0.15 g/L Ni, 0.7 g/L Al, and 0.4 g/L Cu) was taken for the separation study by employing the organic solvents Acorga M5640, Ionquest 801 and their synergistic mixtures (Pranolo et al., 2010). Approximately 99% Cu and 55% Fe were extracted at an equilibrium pH 2.5 and 4.0, respectively with 2 vol.% Acorga M5640. Use of 7 vol. %Ionquest 801 extracted entire Fe and Al at pH 2 and 5, respectively. A subsequent increase in the pH to 6 could extract 60% Cu along with a 10% co-extraction of cobalt. It clearly infers that neither Acorga nor Ionquest alone is e ff ective for complete separation of Cu, Al and Fe. Therefore, a mixture of 2% Acorga M5640 and 7% Ionquest 801 was employed for extracting Cu, Al and Fe by avoiding the co-extraction of cobalt. The organic mixture shifted the copper extraction isotherm (pH50) from 5.45 to 2.0, much lower than that of using Ionquest 801 alone because of the strong a ffi nity of Acorga for the copper. A complete separation of Cu, Al and Fe over Co, Ni and Li was achieved at pH ≤ 4.5 (Fig. 10). Alternatively, the use of PC-88A in a pH range 2.5 -3.0 has been suggested for Al extraction after a prior removal of Cu with Acorga M5640 at pH 1.5 -2.0 (Suzuki et al., 2012).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 3.4.1.2. Using chemical precipitation . The removal of metal impurities like, Cu, Fe, Al, Mn is one of the simple processes, depending mainly upon the solution pH and hydrolysis constant. The study of potential and solubility of metal hydroxide species diagrams as a function of pH is very useful for this (Pourbaix, 1974), which reveals the plausible mechanism for separation of Fe, Cu, Al, and Mn by precipitation at a particular pH. Kang et al., (2010) used a dil. NaOH and CaCO3 solution to adjust the pH to 6.5 and precipitating the hydroxides of Fe, Cu, and Al. In a variance to this, Chen et al., (2011) performed stepwise precipitation of each of the metal impurities. The solution pH values of 3.1 at 95 °C, 4.0 at 70 °C and 5.5 at 30 °C were optimized for the removal of Fe, Mn and Cu, respectively. The (NH4)2S2O8 was used as the precipitating agent for Mn in this study (Chen et al., 2011) which was further modi fi ed by precipitating the MnO2 at pH 2.0 by using KMnO4 as the precipitating reagent (Chen et al., 2015a). A dosage in molar concentration for MnO2:KMnO4 = 1:2 was optimized by Nguyen et al., (2014), while Wang et al., (2009) suggested the heating of the solution to 50 °C for the desired level of precipitation of Mn. A signi fi cant loss of Co along with some Li has been widely reported in precipitation removal of the impurities. As described in the sea water section, the di ff erent forms of MnO2 are used as an adsorbent for lithium and the larger surface area of MnO2 and in -situ precipitated hydroxides of iron are good enough to provide the surfaces for precipitation sorption of cobalt (Mishra et al., 2011; Srivastava et al., 2013a,b). Therefore, the reported losses of lithium and cobalt can be attributed to their sorption onto the precipitates of Fe and Mn. A saturated solution of Na2CO3 has also been used to precipitate the MnCO3 at pH 7.5, leaving Ni, Co and Li in the solution (Nayl et al., 2015).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After the separation of Cu, Al, Fe and Mn from the leach liquor of LIBs, the subsequent removal of cobalt and nickel is needed before recovering the lithium values. The removal process of cobalt and nickel can also be sub-divided into two methodssolvent extraction and chemical precipitation, and these are being discussed below.
texts.text: After the separation of Cu, Al, Fe and Mn from the leach liquor of LIBs, the subsequent removal of cobalt and nickel is needed before recovering the lithium values. The removal process of cobalt and nickel can also be sub-divided into two methodssolvent extraction and chemical precipitation, and these are being discussed below.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 3.4.2.1. Using solvent extraction . Pranolo et al. (2010) used Cyanex 272 to extract cobalt in a pH range 5.5 -6 using 15 vol.% Cyanex-272 diluted in Shellsol D70. A 10% Acorga M5640 was used to extract Co with extraction e ffi ciency more than 85% at pH 7. Negligible extraction of lithium occurs at this pH and hence, separation factor for cobalt/ lithium is not provided. The stripping e ffi ciency of cobalt from the loaded Acorga was not e ff ective even at the higher concentration of sulfuric acid. Therefore, PC-88A and TOA were chosen as the alternative extractant for the extraction of cobalt with fairly high separation factor nearly to 350, 90% cobalt was extraction at eq. pH 4.5 using 10% PC-88A. texts.orig: extractants with and without EDTA. EDTA forms Co-EDTA complex which is hydrophilic in nature, is transferred to the organic phase leading to the decreased distribution ratio of Co 2+ compared to the system without EDTA. Separation factor of Ni and Co at pH 5.0 for di ff erent systems is found to be: 0.05 mol/L PC-88A: 4.99, 0.05 mol/L Cyanex-272: 3.27, 0.05 mol/L PC-88A + EDTA, 286.7; 0.05 mol/L Cyanex-272 + EDTA, 58.47; 0.05 mol/L PC-88A + 0.05 mol/L Cyanex-272 + EDTA, 712.7. Whilst the separation factor for the synergistic system consisting of a mixture of 0.05 mol/L PC88A + 0.05 mol/L Cyanex-272 is observed to be ∼ 5.3 only. This clearly indicates excellent behavior of the mixture PC-88A + Cyanex272 + EDTA for the separation of Ni and Co.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Swain et al. (2007a) separated the cobalt and lithium from sulfate solution containing 0.01 mol/L Co and 0.01 mol/L Li with initial pH 5. Approximately 84% of cobalt was extracted along with 8% lithium at an equilibrium pH of 6.9 using 0.03 mol/L Na-Cyanex 272 into the organic phase. Jha et al. (2013a, 2013b) used Cyanex 272 for the separation of cobalt and lithium from the sulfate solution containing 10.44 g/L Co and 1.33 g/L Li. Almost 99% Co was extracted at equilibrium pH 5 and O/A ratio 1/1 in a two-stage process using 15% Cyanex 272 mixed with 3% isodecanol, the latter acting as a phase modi fi er. However, ∼ 8% Li was also co-extracted along with cobalt which was scrubbed with 10% Na2CO3 at O/A ratio 1/1. After separation of cobalt, ra ffi nate contains pure lithium solution which can be recovered as lithium carbonate. Nguyen et al. (2014) also reported the recovery of cobalt and nickel from the sulfate leach solution containing 25.1 g/L Co, 2.54 g/L Ni and 6.2 g/L Li (pH 2.03) using PC-88A. It was found that more than 99% Co was extracted at equilibrium pH 4.5 using 60% Na-0.56 mol/L PC-88Ain two countercurrent stages at an O/A phase ratio of 3/1 and equilibrium pH of 4.5. Almost 99% Ni could then be extracted at the equilibrium pH 6 using 5%PC-88A. The pure lithium solution obtained undergoes precipitation to recover lithium as lithium carbonate as per Fig. 11 (Eh-pH diagram) at 90 -100 °C due to its lower solubility at the high temperature (Fig. 12). Further, diluted sulfate solution was used for the separation of Ni and Li containing 2.54 g/L Ni and 4.82 g/L Li (pH ∼ 2). More than 99.6% nickel was extracted with 0.15 mol/L PC-88A in two counter-current stages at O/A: 1/1 and pH 6.5 (Nguyen et al., 2015). Kang et al. (2010) also reported the extraction of ∼ 98% Co along with ∼ 1% co-extraction of Ni using 50% saponi fi ed 0.4 mol/L Cyanex 272 at equilibrium pH 6 with the separation factors close to 750 for Co/Li and Co/Ni. If more than 50% saponi fi ed Cyanex-272 was employed for extraction of cobalt, then co-extraction of nickel and lithium also increased.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 3.4.2.2. Using chemical precipitation . Despite superior performance of solvent extraction, a few studies have also been reported to separate the Co and Ni using chemical precipitation by adjusting the pH of solution. Wang et al. (2009) used the di ff erent kind of precipitating agents to separate the metals from the chloride leach liquor. Firstly, manganese was precipitated as manganese dioxide (MnO2) as shown in Fig. 13. Then, a nickel amine complex [Ni(NH3)6] 2+ was formed by adding ammonia at pH 9 which was treated with dimethylglyoxime (DMG) to form a red complex of nickel-DMG. This solid complex is separated and re-dissolved in HCl to get nickel solution and precipitated as nickel hydroxide at pH 11. After recovery of nickel, cobalt remains in mother solution as cobalt hexamine [Co (NH3)6] 3+ was recovered by ammonia neutralization with hydrochloric acid and then precipitated at pH 11 as cobalt hydroxide. Finally a pure solution of lithium was obtained which was treated with sodium carbonate to recover the lithium carbonate at ∼ 100 °C (Fig. 13). This process seems to be very complicated which requires several steps to separate the impurities with lots of reagent consumption. Nayl et al., 2015) reported the separation of metals in the form of their carbonates using sodium carbonate as a precipitating agent while using the sodium hydroxide for adjusting the pH. It was found that 91% Ni was precipitated as nickel carbonate (NiCO3) at pH 9.0 using 2.0 mol/L NaOH. The pH of mother solution was then increased to 11 -12 to remove cobalt as the cobalt hydroxide using NaOH at room temperature. Lithium was fi nally precipitated as Li2CO3 using Na2CO3 at ∼ 90 °C. The precipitate was washed with hot water to eliminate the entrained sodium and was dried at 100 °C for 60 min to get highly pure lithium carbonate. Further economic aspect of recycling of LIBs has been also analyzed as discussed below.

texts.orig: 3.5. Economic aspect of LIBs recycling
texts.text: 3.5. Economic aspect of LIBs recycling

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To analyze the economical aspect of LIBs recycling, various signi fi cant factors like operational cost, transportation fee, utility of the products, scale of the process etc. have been taken into account. The recycling of LIBs would be economic if the revenues generated from recovered materials along with the avoided disposal costs would be greater than the collection and processing costs. Some of the companies e.g. Umicore, Toxco, Inmetco, Recupyl have developed the recycling processes using di ff erent pyro/hydro/combined (pyro + hydro) metallurgical techniques (Foster et al., 2014). To calculate the process economy of these established processes, energy consumption has been taken as a major factor, which would be almost identical in general if practiced anywhere else than the currently being practiced. Another important factor has taken into account is the metal recovered and their values. As it is well-known that pyrometallurgical operation are highcalori fi c process only focused on recovering cobalt and nickel, leaving the lithium and other metals into slag which going to be feed in cement industry. In contrast, the hydrometallurgical operations are comparatively low-calori fi c process capable of recovering lithium and aluminum in addition to high value metals. Notably, pyrometallurgy is a fl exible process capable of accepting a wide variety of LIBs in a single facility (Dunn et al., 2012). Hence, the new recycling technology consists of an integrated physical, pyroand hydro techniques to o ff er low waste generation, reduce in total energy demand with recovery of the maximum metallic values, but yet to tested at commercial level. Therefore, in terms of energy consumption the three operational routes is compared and presented in Table 5a. It can be seen that the hydrometallurgical operation consumes least energy with an ability to recover lithium too. Henceforth, a detail economic analysis based on each steps of life cycle assessment of recycling has been presented in Table 5b. The base of calculation has been made on a 5% recycling rate for year 2016 which is supposed to be increased up to 10% next four years by 2020, thus processing 993.5 t and 3974 t LIBs respectively. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Perspective and recommendations
texts.text: 4. Perspective and recommendations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As projected the increasing demand of energy storage devices will continue to be critical for lithium in the near future and therefore, the e ffi cient exploitation of the non-conventional resources is important. The exploitation of sea water has ocean of opportunities if commercialized properly. However, even the most explored adsorption process is yet to be found fi t for the commercial application. For this, the enhancement in sorption capacity of the designed sorbent/s, low cost of their synthesis and operation, and e ffi cient regeneration are the main points that are to be addressed in the future developments. By reviewing the existing literatures on the adsorption processes, it has been found that obtaining the optimized pH above the normal range of sea water i.e., pH 8.0 -8.5, in the case of other than manganese oxides as the adsorbents may not be practically feasible. The high ionic strength of sea water with exceptionally low lithium concentration per unit volume would never be easy for the pH adjustment. This calls for the search of suitable (modi fi ed) adsorbent that could achieve the maximum sorption of lithium within the prevailing pH range of the sea water, without altering the same (pH).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation of lithium by the means of di ff usion-dialysis from the sea water can be one of the future technologies to be applicable on commercial scale, which is also dependent on how e ffi ciently the durability of membrane can be enhanced. Therefore, maneuvering the membrane for this speci fi c purpose has become more important, and impregnating the membranes with the organic solvents or ionic liquids may be included in the future direction of work in this area. Looking at the minute content of lithium in the sea water, a lithium recovery operation integrated with the desalination plant/ salt production unit/ nuclear fuel coolant system can present a remarkable change in the lithium production strategy. The reduced volume and increased concentration of lithium in the e ffl uents/ output streams can undergo through a di ff usion/ electro-dialysis step. An increased concentration of lithium without any extra e ff orts emanating from the output streams would save the pre-concentration time for lithium production, thereby resulting in the low processing cost. Additionally, it would be bene fi cial by generating extra revenue to those units from which its integration is adopted.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Handling of the spent LIBs is utmost necessary to ensure their entire recycling. The collection and management system in the large consumer developing countries are still a big problem, without that it is impossible to get back all the lithium into the main fl ow of the materials. The lack of facilities for recycling and the practices by the unauthorized sectors are the attributes that could be the cause of main concern for overall sustainability, which can be handled by some appropriate policies (This is not the subject of discussion in this paper). The lower market prices of lithium as compared to the cobalt in the LIBs are another fact due to which the recyclers are not much interested in lithium recovery. The need of high lithium content required for the precipitation of LiCO3 can be overcome by producing the other industrial precursor/s like phosphate salts or, getting concentrated solution using the solvent extraction techniques. This problem can also be tackled by fi xing the lock-cycle operation in appropriate manner while integrating the bleed stream containing high lithium content. The recycler should also be encouraged for zero waste of resources, especially by looking at the strategic criticality of lithium in energy devices and green technologies.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A number of adsorbents like manganese oxide, titanium oxide, alumina, and activated carbon have been used for the lithium enrichment from sea water. The spinel manganese oxides have shown the higher adsorption capacity. The suitable pH for adsorption has been found to be in the alkaline range (pH 8 -12), while desorption by dil. HCl (of pH ∼ 2) has yielded greater recovery of lithium. Electro- dialysis is an emerging technology that can adequately upgrade the lithium contents from the lean concentration of sea water. Lithium ionic superconductor replacing the organic membrane has shown a prominent selectivity for lithium, which can allow only Li + ions to pass through leaving the other cations (K + , Na + , Mg 2+ , Ca 2+ etc.) in another side. The electro-dialysis process has been found to be advantageous due to the superior lithium recovery of ∼ 85% in a-half desorption time (180 min) as compared to that of chemical adsorbents. Enhancing the membrane durability might therefore, be of great consequence for the commercial application of electro-dialysis in the near future.
texts.text: A number of adsorbents like manganese oxide, titanium oxide, alumina, and activated carbon have been used for the lithium enrichment from sea water. The spinel manganese oxides have shown the higher adsorption capacity. The suitable pH for adsorption has been found to be in the alkaline range (pH 8 -12), while desorption by dil. HCl (of pH ∼ 2) has yielded greater recovery of lithium. Electro- dialysis is an emerging technology that can adequately upgrade the lithium contents from the lean concentration of sea water. Lithium ionic superconductor replacing the organic membrane has shown a prominent selectivity for lithium, which can allow only Li + ions to pass through leaving the other cations (K + , Na + , Mg 2+ , Ca 2+ etc.) in another side. The electro-dialysis process has been found to be advantageous due to the superior lithium recovery of ∼ 85% in a-half desorption time (180 min) as compared to that of chemical adsorbents. Enhancing the membrane durability might therefore, be of great consequence for the commercial application of electro-dialysis in the near future.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The recycling process of the LIBs essentially consists of a dismantling and pre-treatment steps for the removal of unwanted metal impurities in the leach liquors, and concentration of target meals (lithium and cobalt) in the black mass. Among all, the pyrolysis has the advantage to be operationalized at commercial level over the ultrasonic cleaning process albeit both can be considered environmental friendly. Sulfuric acid has been commonly used to leach the black mass (LiMxOy) due to its lower cost and lesser corrosiveness with H2O2 to enhance the cobalt leaching in cobaltous form. Whilst the biodegradable organic acids can diminish the evolution of toxic and harmful gases, and hence are found to be promising for leaching of the valuable metals. The separation and puri fi cation processes comprising of the solvent extraction and/or chemical precipitation for lithium recovery (along with cobalt) from the leach liquors are quite simple, as it is commonly practiced in other hydrometallurgical processes. A tendency to form the stable hydrated species of lithium only at a higher equilibrium pH (>7) needs to be maneuvered for the organic extractants by mixing the cation exchangers with neutral extractant of slightly basic property. Precipitation is extensively applied to produce Li2CO3 from the both the sea water and leach liquors generated during the processing of spent LIBs. The developed processes are quite promising which need to be scaled-up for the commercialized operation. The economic analysis of recycling process has indicated hydrometallurgical operation bene fi cial than the pyrometallurgical. The analysis also revealed that as the recycling rate and market demand goes high for the lithium with its main constituent metal cobalt in LIBs, the recycling practice would be move in pro fi t.
